Endocrine Pancreas & Diabetes Drugs — Dr. Ochs

PLO 1–5 Insulin Glucagon DM Types T2DM Path Exercise Diagnosis Insulin Drugs T2DM Drugs 2nd Messengers

Pancreatic Islets of Langerhans PLO 1

The endocrine pancreas consists of the Islets of Langerhans, which make up only 1–1.5% of pancreatic mass but are scattered throughout the exocrine pancreas. Blood supply comes from the celiac artery (most of the pancreas) and the superior mesenteric artery (posterior lobe). First described by pathologist Paul Langerhans in 1869.

Islets of Langerhans — β cells (insulin) located centrally; α cells (glucagon) along periphery

Cell Types and Their Hormones

Cell TypeHormoneChemical NatureFunction
β (beta)Insulin51-AA peptide (A & B chains)Anabolic: ↑ glucose uptake, ↑ glycogenesis, ↑ lipogenesis
β (beta)Amylin (IAPP)PeptideCo-secreted with insulin; slows glucose absorption in duodenum; suppresses glucagon
α (alpha)Glucagon29-AA peptideCatabolic: ↑ glycogenolysis, ↑ gluconeogenesis, ↑ hepatic glucose output
δ (delta)SomatostatinPeptide↓ GI activity; suppresses both glucagon and insulin secretion
PP (γ)Pancreatic polypeptidePeptideMay inhibit exocrine pancreatic secretion & gall bladder contraction; role in nutrient metabolism unknown
ε (epsilon)GhrelinPeptideStimulates appetite, increases food intake, promotes fat storage
High Yield
PP cells are primarily located in the posterior lobe (all other cell types are scattered throughout). Ghrelin is also produced in the stomach and brain. Amylin is deficient in diabetes.

Insulin: Structure, Secretion, Receptor & Actions PLOs 2–5

Insulin Synthesis

Insulin is a 51-amino acid peptide with a 21-AA A chain and 30-AA B chain linked by disulfide bonds. It is synthesized as preproinsulin → proinsulin → insulin + C-peptide. Cleavage is by PCSK1 & PCSK2 (proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin).

C-Peptide — Clinical Pearl
C-peptide is released in equimolar amounts with insulin. It has no biological activity, is not degraded by the liver, and has a half-life 3–4× longer than insulin. It serves as a biomarker of endogenous insulin release. Removed by the kidney.

Secretory Granule Contents

Componentt½Key Facts
Insulin3–5 minFirst-pass removes 50% in liver
C-peptide~15–20 minNo bioactivity; renal clearance; biomarker of endogenous insulin
ProinsulinLongSmall amount secreted intact; 7–8% bioactivity of insulin; makes up 12–20% of immunoreactive insulin

Stimulants & Inhibitors of Insulin Secretion PLO 2

Regulation of insulin secretion from a pancreatic β cell — glucose entry → ↑ ATP → KATP channel closure → depolarization → Ca²⁺ influx → exocytosis
Mechanism of Glucose-Stimulated Insulin Secretion
  1. Glucose enters β cell via bidirectional GLUT1/GLUT3 transporters (NOT GLUT2 in humans!)
  2. Glucose is metabolized → ↑ ATP
  3. ATP binds and inhibits KATP channels (Kir6.2 subunit) → blocks K⁺ efflux
  4. Depolarization of β cell membrane
  5. Opens voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺
  6. Insulin exocytosis
Stimulants of Insulin Release

Direct stimulants:

  • Primary Glucose (most potent)
  • Amino acids (leucine — indirect via ↑ ATP)
  • Acetylcholine (M3 → Gq → PLC → IP3 → Ca²⁺ → PKC)
  • Drugs: Sulfonylureas, meglitinides (bind SUR1 on KATP)

Amplifiers of glucose-induced secretion:

  • GLP-1, GIP (incretins) → Gs → AC → cAMP → PKA/EPAC2
  • CCK, gastrin, secretin
  • β-adrenergic stimulation (minor)
  • Arginine (direct depolarization)
  • Glucagon
  • Drugs: GLP-1 agonists
Inhibitors of Insulin Release
  • α-adrenergic stimulation (epinephrine/NE via α₂ receptors → Gi → ↓ cAMP) — protects against hypoglycemia during exercise
  • Somatostatin (SST2/SST3 → Gi/o)
  • Drugs: thiazides, β-blockers, clonidine
  • Diazoxide (activates SUR1 → opens KATP)
High Yield — Potency of Insulin Secretagogues
Glucose ≫≫ Amino Acids > Free Fatty Acids. FFAs are relatively weak acute secretagogues. Among AAs, leucine primarily stimulates insulin, alanine primarily stimulates glucagon, and arginine stimulates both.

Biphasic Insulin Secretion LO 3

Biphasic insulin secretion pattern
  • Phase 1: Rapid burst of preformed insulin (minutes)
  • Phase 2: Gradual increase to sustained levels (newly synthesized insulin)
  • Sustained stimulation > 4 hours → β cell desensitization to glucose
Time Course
Basal secretion: ~30 units/day. Fasting plasma: ~10 μU/mL. After feeding: >100 μU/mL. Insulin increases within 8–10 min, peaks at 30–45 min, returns to baseline by 90–120 min.

Insulin Receptor & Signaling PLO 3

Insulin signaling — PI3K/AKT (metabolic) and RAS/MAPK (mitogenic) pathways
Insulin Receptor Signaling Cascade
  1. Insulin binds α subunits of the insulin receptor (receptor tyrosine kinase)
  2. Receptor dimerization and autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues on intracellular β subunits
  3. Phosphorylation of tyrosine on IRS (Insulin Receptor Substrate)
  4. Signal splits into two major arms:
    • PI3K → PDK → PKB/AKT pathway = Metabolic (glucose transport, glycogen synthesis, lipogenesis, protein synthesis, ↓ gluconeogenesis)
    • RAS/MAPK pathway (via GRB2 → SOS → RAS) = Mitogenic (cell growth/differentiation)
  5. PKB/AKT → ↑ GLUT4 translocation to cell surface in muscle and adipose tissue

Transcription Regulation (Foxo1 & PPARγ)

Insulin → ↑ AKT → inhibits Foxo1 (a forkhead transcription factor that is active during fasting).

  • Foxo1 promotes (fasting activities): gluconeogenesis, ↑ VLDL, inhibits lipogenesis/glycolysis, inhibits PPARγ
  • Insulin (via AKT) inhibits Foxo1 → net result: ↓ fasting activities, ↑ fed-state activities, ↑ PPARγ activity
PPARγ (Peroxisome Proliferator Activator Receptor-γ)
Expressed in adipose, intestinal, and immune cells. Functions: promotes lipid storage in adipose (vs. ectopic), inhibits pro-inflammatory adipokines/cytokines (↓ insulin resistance), shifts macrophages to anti-inflammatory M2 state from proinflammatory M1. PPARγ is the target of thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone).

Endocrine Effects of Insulin PLO 4

Insulin effects on target tissues
Target TissueInsulin Effects
Liver↑ glycogenesis, ↑ glycolysis, ↑ lipogenesis, ↑ protein synthesis
↓ glycogenolysis, ↓ gluconeogenesis, ↓ ketogenesis
GLUT2 entry is insulin-independent
Skeletal Muscle↑ glucose uptake (GLUT4 translocation — insulin-dependent)
↑ glycogenesis, ↑ protein synthesis, ↑ K⁺ uptake
Major site of glucose disposal
Adipose Tissue↑ glucose uptake (GLUT4), ↑ lipogenesis
↓ lipolysis (insulin is a potent inhibitor of lipolysis)
↑ FFA esterification to TGs
BrainGlucose entry via GLUT1 (BBB) and GLUT3 (neuronal) — insulin-independent
High Yield Summary
Insulin = anabolic. ↑ glycogenesis, glycolysis, lipogenesis. ↓ glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis. Predominates in the fed state. Increases GLUT4 insertion into membranes of muscle and fat.

Glucose Transporter Family

TransporterLocationKey Feature
GLUT1BBB, RBCs, most tissuesHigh affinity → transports at LOW glucose (fasting). Bidirectional. Also in human β cells
GLUT2Liver, intestines, kidneyLow affinity → transports at HIGH glucose. Bidirectional. In RAT β cells only (NOT human!)
GLUT3NeuronsHigh affinity. Also in human β cells
GLUT4Muscle, adipose, heartInsulin-dependent. Sequestered intracellularly; requires insulin for translocation. Exercise also ↑ GLUT4 via AMPK
GLUT5IntestineFructose transporter
SGLT1Intestine, kidney (distal PT)Active Na⁺ co-transporter; 10% of renal glucose reabsorption
SGLT2Kidney (proximal PT)Active Na⁺ co-transporter; 90% of renal glucose reabsorption. Target of SGLT2 inhibitors
High Yield — SGLT2 in Diabetes
In diabetes: ↑ glucose filtered → ↑ SGLT2 expression → ↑ glucose reabsorption → worsens hyperglycemia. SGLT2 inhibitors block this to ↑ glucosuria.

AMPK Signaling

Regulation and function of AMPK
AMPK (5ʼAMP-Activated Protein Kinase)
  • Activated under low energy states (low ATP, high AMP)
  • Exercise activates AMPK (ATP → AMP)
  • AMPK → ↑ catabolic pathways, ↑ insulin-independent glucose uptake via GLUT4
  • Insulin → ↑ AKT → inhibits AMPK in the liver
  • Hyperinsulinemia in early T2DM → ↑ AKT → ↓ AMPK → ↑ FFA synthesis (lipogenesis)
  • Metformin activates AMPK by ↓ mitochondrial ATP production → ↑ AMP
  • Adiponectin also ↑ AMPK signaling

Glucagon & Related Hormones PLO 5

Processing of proglucagon — tissue-specific cleavage in α cells vs. L cells

Proglucagon is processed differently depending on tissue:

  • α cells (PCSK2): → Glucagon, GRPP, major proglucagon fragment
  • L cells (PCSK1/3): → Glicentin, oxyntomodulin, GLP-1, GLP-2

Glucagon

  • Produced by α cells in response to low blood glucose
  • t½ = 3–6 minutes; fasting immunoreactive levels ~75 pg/mL (30–40% is glucagon)
  • Activates glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis → ↑ hepatic glucose production
  • Patients with diabetes continue to produce glucagon
  • Also stimulates rate and force of cardiac contraction, bypassing β-receptors

Glucagon Secretion — Stimulants & Inhibitors

Stimulants
  • Hypoglycemia
  • Amino acids (arginine ↑ glucagon AND insulin; alanine primarily ↑ glucagon)
  • CCK, catecholamines (β₂), acetylcholine
  • Gastrin, GIP, glucocorticoids
  • Fasting, intense exercise
Inhibitors
  • Hyperglycemia (direct and indirect via insulin/somatostatin)
  • Insulin (paracrine from β cells)
  • Somatostatin
  • GABA (released from β cells; α cells have GABA receptors)
  • ↑ Free fatty acid levels
Mechanism by which insulin suppresses glucagon from α cells — KATP channel activation, GABAAR Cl⁻ influx, transcription inhibition

Glucagon Signaling Pathway

Glucagon binds GPCR (class B) → Gαs → adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP → PKA → phosphorylation of glycogen/gluconeogenic enzymes

Pancreatic Paracrine Effects

Paracrine relationships among islet cells
  • β (insulin & GABA) and δ (somatostatin) → ↓ α cells (glucagon)
  • α (glucagon) → ↑ β cells (insulin)
  • δ (somatostatin) → ↓ β cells (insulin)
  • Glucose stimulates β & δ cells; amino acids stimulate β & α cells
  • High-protein meals → primarily α cells → ↑ glucagon

Incretins (GLP-1 & GIP) PLO 5

The incretin effect: oral glucose elicits greater insulin response than IV glucose
FeatureGLP-1GIP
SourceL cells (distal intestinal mucosa)K cells (intestine)
Stimulated byFAs, AAs, oral glucoseFAs, AAs, oral glucose
Insulin effect↑ insulin secretion↑ insulin secretion
Glucagon↓ (suppresses)No significant effect
Gastric emptying↓ (slows)No significant effect on motility
Satiety↑ (decreases food intake)No significant effect
Gastric H⁺—↓ (may be supraphysiological)
DegradationBoth rapidly inactivated by DPP-4 (< 2 min)
High Yield — The Incretin Effect
Oral glucose provokes significantly more insulin than IV glucose at the same glucose levels. This "incretin effect" is blunted in T2DM (only ~50% of normal). This is the therapeutic rationale for GLP-1 agonists and DPP-4 inhibitors.

Diabetes Mellitus: Type 1 vs. Type 2 LOs 1, 2, 6

Fasting vs. prandial state — insulin and glucagon roles in glucose homeostasis
Comparison of Type 1 vs. Type 2 DM
FeatureType 1 DMType 2 DM
MechanismInsulin deficiency — autoimmune β cell destructionInsulin resistance ± progressive β cell failure
OnsetUsually childhood/young adultUsually adult (increasingly younger)
% of DM~5–10%>90% in the US
Body habitusUsually thin/normalUsually obese (↑ abdominal fat)
Insulin levelsNone (absolute deficiency)High initially (hyperinsulinemia), then low in advanced disease
Insulin sensitivityHigh (receptors work)Low (resistance)
KetosisCommon — DKA is presenting feature in 20–40%Rare (if occurs → usually infection/trauma)
GeneticsHLA-DR3 & HLA-DR4; concordance 25–50% in twinsStrong genetic link; >90% concordance in monozygotic twins
HistologyIslet leukocytic infiltratesIslet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP/amylin) deposits
TreatmentExogenous insulin required for survivalLifestyle + oral agents ± insulin
PresentationPolydipsia, polyuria, polyphagia, weight loss, DKAOften asymptomatic; acanthosis nigricans; large waist
Insulin Deficiency vs. Insulin Resistance

Insulin deficiency (T1DM): β cell destruction → no insulin production → cannot suppress glucagon → uncontrolled hepatic glucose output + no peripheral glucose uptake + lipolysis → ketogenesis → DKA.

Insulin resistance (T2DM): tissues have ↓ responsiveness to insulin → compensatory hyperinsulinemia → eventually β cells cannot keep up → progressive ↑ glucose. Hepatic insulin resistance + hyperglucagonemia → ↑ hepatic glucose production = hallmark fasting hyperglycemia.

T2DM Pathophysiology & Obesity LO 4

Obesity & Insulin Resistance

  • Majority of T2DM patients have excess adiposity
  • Those without BMI >30 still have excessive abdominal fat distribution (high waist-to-hip ratio)
  • WHO abdominal obesity: waist-to-hip ratio ≥0.90 (men), ≥0.85 (women)
  • Large waist circumference (>40" men, >35" women) ↑ risk for any BMI
  • Obesity is the most common cause of insulin resistance

Insulin Resistance: Tissue-Specific Effects

TissueEffect of Insulin Resistance
Liver↑ Basal hepatic glucose production → fasting hyperglycemia (hallmark). Failure to suppress glucagon (may even rise after meals) = hyperglucagonemia
MuscleMajor site of glucose disposal. In T2DM, glucose uptake ↓ by 50% due to impaired signaling. Compensatory hyperinsulinemia may contribute to atherosclerosis
Adipocyte↑ Basal FFA levels; fail to suppress after glucose ingestion. Chronically ↑ FFAs → insulin resistance in muscle & liver, impaired insulin secretion
Plasma insulin & glucose rise together until fasting glucose ~140 mg/dL; beyond that, β cells produce progressively less insulin

Mechanisms of Insulin Resistance

1. Adipokines

Adipokines are cytokines released by adipocytes that target insulin signaling pathways:

↓ Insulin Signaling (Bad)↑ Insulin Signaling (Good)
TNFα, IL-6, leptin, resistinAdiponectin (also ↑ AMPK)

Obesity increases the bad adipokines and decreases adiponectin.

2. Ectopic Fat Storage

Excess adipose releases fat that accumulates in muscle, liver, β cells → ↓ insulin sensitivity (lipotoxicity).

3. Inflammation

Pro-inflammatory adipokines recruit macrophages → more cytokines → ↓ insulin sensitivity

Pro-inflammatory adipokines recruit macrophages to adipose tissue → activated macrophages release TNFα, IL-6, NO → further ↓ adipocyte insulin sensitivity → vicious cycle.

4. PPARγ Dysfunction

In T2DM, ↓ insulin → ↑ Foxo1 → ↓ PPARγ → loss of all PPARγ protective functions: ↓ insulin signaling, ↓ adipose storage, ↑ ectopic storage, ↑ pro-inflammatory cytokines, M1 macrophage activation.

5. β Cell Defects

Progression of β cell failure in T2DM
  • ↓ Phase 1 insulin and maximal insulin secretion
  • Insulin resistance alone does NOT cause T2DM — many obese individuals don't develop it
  • Likely a genetic predisposition that fails to compensate (fails to ↑ β cell number)
  • Pro-inflammatory cytokines → ↑ β cell loss in susceptible individuals
  • T2DM has >90% concordance in monozygotic twins (strong genetic link)

Exercise & Glucose Uptake LO 5

Exercise-stimulated vs. insulin-stimulated glucose uptake pathways — both use GLUT4 but are distinct signaling cascades
Why Exercise is Critical in Diabetes Management
  • Muscle glucose uptake is primarily via GLUT4
  • Contraction-stimulated pathway is distinct from the insulin pathway
  • Exercise activates AMPK → GLUT4 translocation (via CaMK, LKB1 — NOT PI3K/AKT)
  • This pathway is functional despite insulin resistance or insulin deficiency
  • Therefore exercise provides glucose uptake even when insulin signaling is impaired
  • Crucial for optimal hyperglycemia control in both T1DM and T2DM

Diagnosis of DM LOs 6, 7

Diagnostic criteria for diabetes mellitus
Diagnostic Criteria
TestNormalPrediabetesDiabetes
Fasting Plasma Glucose<100 mg/dL100–125 mg/dL≥126 mg/dL
2-hr OGTT<140 mg/dL140–199 mg/dL≥200 mg/dL
HbA1c<5.7%5.7–6.4%≥6.5%
Random Plasma Glucose—≥200 mg/dL + symptoms

HbA1c (Glycosylated Hemoglobin)

  • Biomarker of average long-term glucose control over last 8–12 weeks
  • Hemoglobin in RBCs is constantly exposed to glucose; glycosylation is proportional to plasma glucose
  • Reflects the life cycle of the RBC (~120 days)
  • Cannot be used for daily insulin adjustments (doesn't reflect acute changes)
  • Reflects efficacy of diet, exercise, and medications on glycemic control
  • Check A1c every 3 months when adjusting medications
Clinical Presentation Differences

T1DM: 20–40% present with DKA after days of polydipsia, polyuria, polyphagia, weight loss.

T2DM: Often asymptomatic. Signs include acanthosis nigricans (hyperpigmented, hyperkeratotic skin in axilla/groin/neck — sign of significant insulin resistance; ↑ insulin binds IGF receptor on melanin-containing cells).

Lab differentiator: C-peptide levels (low/absent in T1DM, normal/high in early T2DM), autoantibodies (anti-GAD, anti-islet cell — present in T1DM).

Medications Affecting Glycemic Control LO 8

Drugs That Can Worsen Hyperglycemia
  • Thiazide diuretics — inhibit insulin release
  • β-blockers — inhibit insulin release, mask hypoglycemia symptoms
  • Clonidine (α₂ agonist) — inhibits insulin release
  • Glucocorticoids — ↑ insulin resistance, ↑ gluconeogenesis
  • Diazoxide — opens KATP channels → inhibits insulin secretion
Pharmacology

Insulin Preparations LOs 9, 10, 11

Human insulin is produced using recombinant DNA technology. Most preparations contain 100 units/mL (newer: 200, 300, 500 U/mL). Administered SC (or continuous SC infusion pump). IV infusion for DKA management. Degraded in liver, muscle, and kidney (~15–20% of endogenous metabolism in kidney; up to 65% for exogenous).

Insulin types — onset, peak, and duration
CategoryAgent(s)OnsetPeakDurationKey Notes
Rapid-actingAspart (Novolog/Fiasp), Lispro (Humalog/Admelog), Glulisine (Apidra)<15 min1–2 hr3–5 hrDon't form hexamers → faster onset. Give 15 min before or right after meal. Preferred over regular to reduce "stacking"
Short-actingRegular (Humulin R, Novolin R)30–60 min2–3 hr6–8 hrGive ~30 min before meal. More late hypoglycemia
IntermediateNPH (Humulin N, Novolin N)2–4 hr4–14 hr10–16 hrCrystalline suspension with protamine; tissue enzymes degrade protamine to release insulin
Long-actingGlargine (Lantus, Basaglar, Toujeo)4–5 hrFlat (no peak)~24 hrSoluble in acid, precipitates at body pH → slow dissolution. Once daily. Don't mix with other insulins
Ultra long-actingDegludec (Tresiba)30–90 minFlat42 hrForms multihexameric chains SC → very slow dissolution. Once daily. Don't mix
InhaledInhaled insulin (Afrezza)<15 min—ShortUltra-rapid dry powder. Not substitute for long-acting. Not for DKA
How Analogs Alter Pharmacokinetics

Regular insulin exists as hexamers in zinc — must dissociate before absorption.

Rapid-acting: AA modifications disrupt hexamer formation → quick dissociation to monomers. Lispro: Pro⇄Lys at B28-B29. Aspart: Pro→Asp at B28. Glulisine: Asn→Lys at B3, Lys→Glu at B29.

Long-acting (Glargine): 2 Arg added to B-chain C-terminal + Gly for Asn at A21 → soluble in acid, precipitates at physiological pH → slow crystalline depot dissolution.

Ultra long-acting (Degludec): Thr removed at B30, Lys B29 conjugated to hexadecanoic acid → forms massive multihexameric chains SC → ultra-slow dissolution + albumin binding.

High Yield — Insulin MOA & Second Messenger
All insulins: bind insulin receptor (receptor tyrosine kinase) → autophosphorylation → IRS → PI3K/AKT pathway (metabolic) and RAS/MAPK pathway (mitogenic). Second messenger pathway: tyrosine kinase → IRS → PI3K → AKT → GLUT4 translocation. No traditional "second messenger" like cAMP — it's a kinase cascade.

Glucagon (Pharmaceutical) LO 12

  • Available as pharmaceutical prep for severe hypoglycemia (SC injection)
  • Baqsimi — first and only glucagon nasal spray (approved 2019)
  • MOA: binds glucagon receptor (class B GPCR → Gαs → ↑ adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP → PKA) → ↑ glycogenolysis & gluconeogenesis → ↑ hepatic glucose output
  • Also stimulates cardiac rate and contractility, bypassing β-receptors — useful in β-blocker-induced cardiac depression

Drugs for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus LOs 13, 14, 15

1. GLP-1 Receptor Agonists GLP-1 RA

Agents: Liraglutide (Victoza), Dulaglutide (Trulicity), Semaglutide (Ozempic SC, Rybelsus PO), Exenatide (Byetta, Bydureon)

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: GLP-1 receptor agonist → Gs → ↑ cAMP → PKA/EPAC2
  • ↑ Insulin secretion (glucose-dependent)
  • ↓ Glucagon secretion
  • ↓ Gastric emptying
  • ↑ Satiety → weight loss (up to 27 lbs with semaglutide)

PK: SC administration (except Rybelsus PO). Renal insufficiency prolongs t½ of exenatide.

Semaglutide and high-dose liraglutide (Saxenda/Wegovy) also FDA-approved for obesity.

2. SGLT2 Inhibitors SGLT2i

Agents: Bexagliflozin (Brenzavvy), Canagliflozin (Invokana), Dapagliflozin (Farxiga), Empagliflozin (Jardiance), Ertugliflozin (Steglatro)

Memory tip: "-gliflozin" has gl + flo because they make the urine flow!

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Inhibit SGLT2 in proximal tubule → block renal glucose reabsorption → ↑ glucosuria
  • Second messenger: None — direct transporter blockade (no receptor signaling)
  • In T2DM, renal glucose resorptive capacity is increased → SGLT2i counteracts this

PK: >65% bioavailable; metabolized by UGT (O-glucuronidation, Phase II); excreted in feces and urine.

First-line for patients with HF or CKD (independent of A1c). Alternative first-line for ASCVD.

3. Biguanide: Metformin Biguanide

Metformin (Glucophage) — traced back to Galega officinalis (goat's rue) used since 1772.

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Activates AMPK (by ↓ mitochondrial ATP production → ↑ AMP)
  • AMPK activation → ↑ fatty acid oxidation, ↑ glucose uptake, ↑ nonoxidative glucose metabolism, ↓ lipogenesis, ↓ gluconeogenesis
  • Second messenger: ↑ AMP → AMPK activation
  • Also ↑ peripheral glucose uptake (mechanism not well understood)

PK: t½ ~1.5–3 hr; NOT metabolized; excreted unchanged via kidney.

1st-line agent for glycemic control in T2DM (glucose-centric algorithm).

Risk of lactic acidosis (inhibits pyruvate carboxylase → ↑ lactate).

4. Dual Incretin (GIP/GLP-1 RA): Tirzepatide Twincretin

Tirzepatide (Mounjaro) — first-in-class.

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Dual agonist of GIP receptor + GLP-1 receptor → Gs → ↑ cAMP → PKA
  • ↑ Insulin secretion, ↓ glucagon, ↓ gastric emptying, ↑ satiety
  • Significantly more weight loss than GLP-1 agonists alone (up to 25 lbs)

PK: SC; t½ ~5 days. FDA-approved for T2DM (May 2022), obesity (Nov 2023), OSA (Dec 2024).

5. Sulfonylureas SU

Agents: Glipizide, Glyburide, Glimepiride

Memory tip: Sulfonylureas start with S, so they start with "gl"

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Bind SUR1 subunit of KATP channels on β cells → close KATP channels → depolarization → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin exocytosis
  • Second messenger: ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ (via voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels from depolarization)
  • Insulin-dependent mechanism — requires functioning β cells
  • Glipizide is pancreatic-specific (SUR1); glimepiride & glyburide are non-specific

PK: Hepatic metabolism; renal & biliary excretion. May need dose adjustment with renal impairment.

6. Thiazolidinedione (TZD) TZD

Pioglitazone (Actos)

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Bind and activate PPARγ (nuclear receptor on fat and vascular cells)
  • Second messenger: Nuclear receptor → gene transcription (no classic 2nd messenger)
  • ↑ Preadipocyte differentiation → mature fat cells (subcutaneous)
  • ↑ Adipose FFA storage (↓ TGs, ↑ HDL), ↓ ectopic lipid storage
  • ↑ Tissue insulin sensitivity (adipose, liver, muscle)
  • ↑ Glucose transporter expression → ↑ glucose uptake
  • Suppress hepatic glucose production
  • ↓ Pro-inflammatory cytokines, shift macrophages M1 → M2

7. DPP-4 Inhibitors DPP-4i

Agents: Sitagliptin (Januvia), Saxagliptin (Onglyza), Linagliptin (Tradjenta), Alogliptin (Nesina)

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Inhibit DPP-4 enzyme → prevent breakdown of endogenous GLP-1 & GIP → ↑ incretin levels
  • Second messenger: Indirect — preserved GLP-1/GIP → ↑ cAMP in β cells
  • ↓ Glucagon production
  • ↑ Insulin response to high glucose
  • Do NOT alter gastric emptying
  • No significant effect on satiety (minimal weight effect)
DPP-4 inhibition preserves intact GLP-1 and GIP from enzymatic degradation

8. Pramlintide (Amylin Analog)

Pramlintide (Symlin)

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Amylin analog — activates amylin receptors (CT receptor + RAMP)
  • Suppresses glucagon secretion, slows gastric emptying, ↑ satiety
  • May lead to weight loss
  • Second messenger: ↑ cAMP (via CT receptor coupling to Gs)
  • Indication: T1DM or T2DM in patients using insulin but without optimal control (add-on therapy)
  • SC injection prior to meals

9. Meglitinides (Short-acting Secretagogues)

Repaglinide (Prandin), Nateglinide (Starlix)

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Same as sulfonylureas — bind SUR1 on KATP channels (lower affinity) → close KATP → depolarization → Ca²⁺ → insulin release
  • Second messenger: ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺
  • Require glucose to stimulate secretion; as glucose normalizes, secretion diminishes
  • Rapidly absorbed, rapid-acting; t½ ~1–1.5 hr; hepatic metabolism
  • Take before each meal; skip dose if meal skipped

10. Alpha-Glucosidase (AG) Inhibitors

Acarbose (Precose), Miglitol (Glyset)

Mechanism & Effects
  • MOA: Competitively inhibit brush border α-glucosidase enzymes (maltase, isomaltase, sucrase, glucoamylase) in small intestine
  • Second messenger: None — direct enzyme inhibition (no receptor)
  • Delay breakdown of complex carbohydrates and sucrose → ↓ postprandial glucose rise
  • PK: Partially absorbed; acarbose eliminated in feces; miglitol & acarbose metabolites via kidneys

Second Messengers Summary LO 15

Drug/ClassTargetSecond Messenger / Signaling
InsulinInsulin receptor (RTK)Tyrosine kinase → IRS → PI3K/AKT (metabolic) + RAS/MAPK (mitogenic)
Liraglutide, Dulaglutide, SemaglutideGLP-1 receptor (GPCR, Gs)↑ cAMP → PKA / EPAC2
TirzepatideGIP + GLP-1 receptors (GPCR, Gs)↑ cAMP → PKA
Bexagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin, ErtugliflozinSGLT2 transporterNone — direct transporter blockade
MetforminMitochondria → AMPK↑ AMP → AMPK activation
Glipizide, Glyburide, GlimepirideSUR1 on KATP channel (β cell)↑ Intracellular Ca²⁺ (via depolarization → VGCC)
PioglitazonePPARγ (nuclear receptor)Gene transcription (nuclear receptor, no classic 2nd messenger)
Sitagliptin, Saxagliptin, Linagliptin, AlogliptinDPP-4 enzymeIndirect ↑ cAMP (via preserved GLP-1/GIP)
PramlintideAmylin receptor (CT + RAMP)↑ cAMP (Gs-coupled)
Repaglinide, NateglinideSUR1 on KATP channel (β cell)↑ Intracellular Ca²⁺ (via depolarization → VGCC)
Acarbose, Miglitolα-glucosidase enzymes (brush border)None — direct enzyme inhibition

Drug Class Identification LO 13

Drug ClassAgentsKey Suffix/Pattern
GLP-1 Receptor AgonistsLiraglutide, Dulaglutide, Semaglutide, Exenatide-glutide / -natide
SGLT2 InhibitorsBexagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin, Ertugliflozin-gliflozin
BiguanideMetforminOnly one
Dual Incretin (GIP/GLP-1 RA)TirzepatideFirst-in-class
SulfonylureasGlipizide, Glyburide, GlimepirideGli-/Gly-
ThiazolidinedionePioglitazone-glitazone
DPP-4 InhibitorsSitagliptin, Saxagliptin, Linagliptin, Alogliptin-gliptin
Amylin AnalogPramlintideOnly one used
MeglitinidesRepaglinide, Nateglinide-glinide
Alpha-Glucosidase InhibitorsAcarbose, MiglitolAcarbose, Miglitol
Comparative overview of diabetes drug classes
